completeness_note: Representative entries only. This catalog is not a complete PMP glossary and does not authorize production publication.

entries.definition: A temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique result and enable intended value.
entries.common_confusion: A recurring operational process can produce deliverables but is not temporary or unique in the same way.
entries.exam_trap: Treating any organized work as a project without checking temporary and unique characteristics.

entries.definition: A coordinated group of related projects and activities managed together to obtain benefits unavailable from separate management.
entries.common_confusion: A large project is not automatically a program.
entries.exam_trap: Choosing program governance solely because work is complex or long.

entries.definition: Projects, programs, and other work grouped to support strategic objectives and investment decisions.
entries.common_confusion: Portfolio components need not be operationally related.
entries.exam_trap: Assuming portfolio management coordinates shared delivery dependencies like program management.

entries.definition: Ongoing, repeatable work that sustains the organization, products, or services.
entries.common_confusion: Operational improvement may be delivered through a project, but the resulting ongoing process remains operations.
entries.exam_trap: Keeping a project open indefinitely because its output continues to operate.

entries.definition: An organizational structure that standardizes, supports, controls, or directs project-management practices according to its mandate.
entries.common_confusion: PMO authority varies; not every PMO directs project managers.
entries.exam_trap: Escalating every decision to the PMO without checking governance and authority.

entries.definition: The justification for an initiative based on expected value, feasibility, costs, benefits, risks, and alternatives.
entries.common_confusion: The charter authorizes a project; the business case explains why it should be pursued.
entries.exam_trap: Using the business case as the detailed execution plan.

entries.definition: A hierarchical decomposition of the total project scope into manageable components.
entries.common_confusion: A WBS organizes scope, not calendar dates or the organization chart.
entries.exam_trap: Treating an activity list or schedule as the scope baseline.

entries.definition: The longest-duration path through a schedule network that determines the earliest project completion under the current model.
entries.common_confusion: Critical does not mean most important or most expensive.
entries.exam_trap: Assuming the critical path never changes after schedule updates.

entries.definition: The budgeted value of work actually completed at a point in time.
entries.common_confusion: EV is not the amount spent and is not the planned value of scheduled work.
entries.exam_trap: Comparing EV to budget at completion without considering the question being asked.

entries.definition: The amount and type of risk an organization is willing to pursue or retain in support of objectives.
entries.common_confusion: Appetite is broader than a specific measurable risk threshold.
entries.exam_trap: Applying the project manager's personal tolerance instead of organizational guidance.

entries.definition: A Scrum accountability focused on establishing Scrum, improving team effectiveness, coaching, and removing organizational impediments.
entries.common_confusion: The Scrum Master is not the team's task-assigning project manager.
entries.exam_trap: Making the Scrum Master the owner of product priority or individual work assignments.

entries.definition: An ordered, evolving collection of work needed to improve a product and pursue product goals.
entries.common_confusion: A backlog is not a frozen predictive scope baseline.
entries.exam_trap: Treating every requested item as committed work regardless of value, readiness, or priority.

entries.definition: Work that has started but has not yet reached the defined completion point in a flow system.
entries.common_confusion: Starting more work can reduce flow rather than increase throughput.
entries.exam_trap: Optimizing individual utilization while ignoring queue growth and cycle time.

entries.definition: The governed use of AI with human accountability and controls for privacy, bias, transparency, safety, security, copyright, and impact.
entries.common_confusion: Human review alone is not sufficient if data, authority, or audit controls are absent.
entries.exam_trap: Delegating final accountability to an AI system because its recommendation appears confident.
